content.text: When a Python agent needs to saturate multiple CPU cores — embedding a million text chunks, parsing enormous files in parallel, or running isolated code sandboxes — spawning separate processes is the only way to achieve true parallelism. The standard library's

both distribute work across child processes, each with its own Python interpreter and memory space, bypassing the Global Interpreter Lock that otherwise limits single-process concurrency to one CPU core at a time.

Python multiprocessing is a concurrency model in which work is distributed across multiple independent OS processes instead of threads or coroutines. Each spawned process is a complete Python interpreter with its own heap, its own GIL, and its own copy of the address space. Because the processes are truly separate, they execute Python bytecode simultaneously on different CPU cores, achieving genuine parallelism that threads cannot.
The two primary entry points are the low-level
, and shared-memory primitives — and the higher-level

API and is the preferred choice when submitting independent tasks. For agentic systems, multiprocessing is the right tool for batch CPU-bound pipelines and isolation boundaries; it is a complement to asyncio, not a replacement.

The GIL prevents more than one thread from executing Python bytecode at a time in a single process. For I/O-bound work — network calls, disk reads — this is largely irrelevant because threads spend most of their time waiting. For CPU-bound work such as text embedding, chunk parsing, or numerical scoring, threads provide no speedup at all. Without multiprocessing, a four-core machine running a CPU-intensive batch pipeline uses exactly one core.

In production agentic systems this matters at multiple pipeline stages. Knowledge-base ingestion — chunking documents, running local embedding models, computing token counts — can take hours on large corpora when single-threaded. Multiprocessing can cut that time by a factor close to the number of available cores. Isolated code-execution sandboxes are another common use case: a crashed worker cannot corrupt the main agent process, and OS resource limits can be applied per child process independently.
— Multiple processes execute Python bytecode simultaneously on separate cores. The GIL inside each process is independent, so there is no shared bottleneck.
— A worker crash, memory leak, or segfault in a native extension cannot corrupt the main process or other workers.
— Untrusted tool output can be executed in a subprocess with OS resource limits (ulimit, cgroups, seccomp) applied only to that child process.
— Each worker's memory is bounded separately; a worker that OOMs is killed by the OS kernel without affecting peers.
— loop.run_in_executor() offloads CPU work to a process pool without blocking the event loop, keeping the serving layer responsive while batch work runs in parallel.
— ProcessPoolExecutor.map() and submit() require no changes to the worker function signature beyond picklability, making adoption straightforward.
— Spawning a fresh Python interpreter takes 100–500 ms. Pools amortise this over many tasks, but per-request process spawning is prohibitively expensive for low-latency APIs.
— Arguments and return values must be pickled to cross the process boundary. For large numpy arrays or complex object graphs, pickling can dominate the actual compute time.
— Workers cannot access Python objects in the main process directly. Shared state requires external stores (Redis, a database) or OS-level shared memory.
— Each process carries its own interpreter overhead (~20–50 MB baseline). Loading large models per worker multiplies memory use by the worker count.
— Lambda functions, closures over non-trivial state, and many class instances are not picklable. Worker functions must be importable top-level definitions.
— The default start method is fork on Linux and spawn on macOS (3.8+) and Windows. Fork is faster but can deadlock if the forked process inherits a locked threading mutex; spawn is safer but slower.

CPython protects its internal reference counts with a single lock — the GIL — so only one thread executes Python bytecode at any moment, even on multi-core hardware. The GIL is released during blocking I/O and certain C extension calls (NumPy matrix operations release it, for example), but for pure Python computation it is a hard serialisation point that makes threads useless for CPU parallelism.

Processes have entirely independent interpreters and therefore independent GILs. The OS kernel schedules processes on separate cores without any coordination at the Python level. This is why multiprocessing is the only standard-library path to CPU parallelism in CPython; alternatives like subinterpreters (PEP 684) exist experimentally in Python 3.12+ but are not yet widely used in production.

is created, Python forks the parent process on Linux or starts fresh interpreters via the spawn method on macOS and Windows. The pool keeps these processes alive and communicates with them via internal OS pipes. Each worker runs a loop that reads a pickled task, executes the function, pickles the return value, and writes it back to the result pipe. When the executor context manager exits, it sends shutdown signals and joins all worker processes.

tuple. The initializer runs once in each worker process at startup — before any tasks are dispatched. This is the correct place to load expensive resources such as ML models, database connections, or compiled regex patterns so they are paid for once per worker, not once per task.
Arguments and results cross the process boundary through pickling. Python's

protocol serialises an object to bytes, writes the bytes to an OS pipe, and the receiving process deserialises them back into a Python object. For large payloads this is expensive: a 100 MB numpy array pickled and sent through a pipe takes tens of milliseconds before actual work begins. The practical guideline is to pass small identifiers (file paths, chunk IDs, database keys) and have workers fetch or load data themselves.

(Python 3.8+) avoids pickling entirely by mapping the same physical memory block into multiple processes. Workers attach to the shared block by name, read the data without copying, and write results back. This is the most efficient IPC for large read-heavy numerical workloads. Other options include
The diagram below shows how a

fits into an agent runtime. The main process holds the event loop and orchestration logic. It submits tasks with pickled arguments through internal OS pipes to worker processes. Each worker runs the CPU-bound function and sends pickled results back. The OS scheduler runs worker processes on separate cores in true parallel. The main process collects results via Future objects.

attrs.alt: ProcessPoolExecutor architecture: main process submits pickled tasks to workers running on separate OS cores, results flow back via pipes

content.text: The dashed return path from Results to the Main Process highlights that the main process is the single point that aggregates output. Workers never communicate directly with each other — all inter-worker coordination must go through shared external state or be designed out of the algorithm entirely.

Multiprocessing is the right choice when the work is CPU-bound, the unit of work is large enough to amortise pickle and spawn overhead, and the payloads crossing the process boundary are small relative to the computation performed.
— embedding 1M document chunks with a local sentence-transformer model, where each chunk takes milliseconds of CPU.
— PDF rendering, HTML extraction, or AST analysis at scale where pure-Python parsing dominates.
— executing agent-generated or user-provided code with timeouts and resource limits, isolated from the main agent process.
— re-ranking many candidate documents with a cross-encoder model.
— offline indexing, preprocessing, or data transformation pipelines where latency is irrelevant but throughput matters.
— spawning a process per HTTP request adds 100–500 ms of latency; use asyncio for I/O-bound request handling.
— if workers spend most time waiting on network or disk, asyncio coroutines or threads are far more memory-efficient.
— pickle and IPC overhead dominates; batch many items into each task unit before submitting.
— when workers need to share and mutate complex in-memory structures, the process boundary makes coordination error-prone.
Step 1 — write a picklable worker function

The worker function must be a module-level function (not a lambda or a closure over non-picklable state). Avoid capturing large objects. Pass small arguments and load resources inside the function or via an initializer.

# worker.py  (module-level, importable by child processes)
import os

_MODEL = None  # populated by initializer, not passed per call


def init_worker(model_path: str) -> None:
    """Run once in each worker at pool startup."""
    global _MODEL
    from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
    _MODEL = SentenceTransformer(model_path)
    print(f"Worker PID {os.getpid()} loaded model")


def embed_chunk(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """CPU-bound work; _MODEL already loaded in this process."""
    return _MODEL.encode(text, normalize_embeddings=True).tolist()
Step 2 — create the pool with an initializer
when creating the executor so heavy resources are loaded once per worker. Set
maps to the number of CPUs, which may exceed available RAM for large models.

from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
from worker import embed_chunk, init_worker

MODEL_PATH = "BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5"

def run_batch_embedding(chunks: list[str], workers: int = 4) -> list[list[float]]:
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(
        max_workers=workers,
        initializer=init_worker,
        initargs=(MODEL_PATH,),
    ) as pool:
        # chunksize batches multiple items per IPC round-trip for better throughput
        vectors = list(pool.map(embed_chunk, chunks, chunksize=64))
    return vectors
An async agent's event loop must not block. Offload process-pool work with
. The function submitted must be synchronous — wrap the
usage with a CPU-bound task and compares wall-clock time against single-threaded execution.

"""parallel_primes.py — Run: python parallel_primes.py"""
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
import math, time


def is_prime(n: int) -> bool:
    if n < 2:
        return False
    if n == 2:
        return True
    if n % 2 == 0:
        return False
    for i in range(3, math.isqrt(n) + 1, 2):
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    return True


def main():
    numbers = list(range(10_000_000, 10_001_000))  # 1 000 candidates

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    seq = [is_prime(n) for n in numbers]
    seq_time = time.perf_counter() - t0

    t1 = time.perf_counter()
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as pool:
        par = list(pool.map(is_prime, numbers, chunksize=50))
    par_time = time.perf_counter() - t1

    assert seq == par  # correctness check
    print(f"Primes found: {sum(par)}")
    print(f"Sequential : {seq_time:.3f}s")
    print(f"Parallel   : {par_time:.3f}s  ({seq_time / par_time:.1f}x speedup)")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Example 2 — async agent with persistent process pool
This example demonstrates the production pattern: a persistent pool created at startup, offloaded from asyncio via
, with graceful shutdown. It simulates the embedding batch flow used in RAG ingestion.

"""async_embed_agent.py — Run: python async_embed_agent.py"""
import asyncio, atexit, hashlib, os, time
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor


# ── Module-level worker function (must be importable) ────────────────────────

def _fake_embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """Simulate CPU-bound embedding with a deterministic hash vector."""
    digest = hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).digest()
    return [b / 255.0 for b in digest[:8]]  # 8-dim demo vector


# ── Agent ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class EmbedAgent:
    def __init__(self, workers: int | None = None):
        self._pool = ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=workers or os.cpu_count())
        atexit.register(self._pool.shutdown, wait=False, cancel_futures=True)

    async def embed_batch(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
        loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()

        def _run() -> list[list[float]]:
            return list(self._pool.map(_fake_embed, texts, chunksize=16))

        return await loop.run_in_executor(None, _run)


# ── Driver ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

async def main():
    agent = EmbedAgent()
    docs = [f"Chunk {i}: content goes here." for i in range(400)]

    print(f"Embedding {len(docs)} chunks across {os.cpu_count()} cores...")
    t0 = time.perf_counter()

    # Both batches run concurrently — event loop is free during process work
    first, second = await asyncio.gather(
        agent.embed_batch(docs[:200]),
        agent.embed_batch(docs[200:]),
    )

    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    vectors = first + second
    print(f"Done: {len(vectors)} vectors, dim={len(vectors[0])}, time={elapsed:.2f}s")
    print("Event loop stayed responsive — concurrent gather proved it.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
A production RAG platform ingests a new document corpus nightly. The pipeline runs as a dedicated Python service, separate from the live agent API, using a
with max_workers set to the number of physical cores (not hyperthreads, to avoid memory bandwidth contention when running dense vector computations with BLAS).

Each worker loads the embedding model once via an initializer, keeping model weights in the worker's heap for the lifetime of the pool. The main process reads document chunks from a database cursor in batches of 256 and feeds them to the pool. Resulting vectors are written to a Qdrant vector store. The architecture achieves near-linear speedup up to around eight cores before the embedding model's internal BLAS threads begin saturating memory bandwidth.

"""nightly_ingest.py — simplified production embedding pipeline"""
import os
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
import psycopg2
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
from qdrant_client.models import PointStruct

EMBED_MODEL = "BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5"
_model = None


def _load_model() -> None:
    global _model
    from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
    _model = SentenceTransformer(EMBED_MODEL)


def embed_row(row: tuple[int, str]) -> tuple[int, list[float]]:
    chunk_id, text = row
    return chunk_id, _model.encode(text, normalize_embeddings=True).tolist()


def main() -> None:
    conn = psycopg2.connect(os.environ["DB_URL"])
    qdrant = QdrantClient(url=os.environ["QDRANT_URL"])
    cur = conn.cursor(name="chunks")
    cur.execute("SELECT id, text FROM chunks WHERE embedded = FALSE")

    workers = min(8, os.cpu_count() or 4)
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=workers, initializer=_load_model) as pool:
        while True:
            rows = cur.fetchmany(256)
            if not rows:
                break
            results = list(pool.map(embed_row, rows, chunksize=32))
            qdrant.upsert(
                collection_name="docs",
                points=[PointStruct(id=cid, vector=vec, payload={}) for cid, vec in results],
            )
            conn.execute(
                "UPDATE chunks SET embedded = TRUE WHERE id = ANY(%s)",
                ([r[0] for r in results],),
            )
            conn.commit()

    print("Ingestion complete.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

The critical design choice is keeping the embedding model in the worker (not the main process) and passing only lightweight tuple payloads across the process boundary. Pickling model weights on every task submission would be orders of magnitude slower.
Mistake 1 — pickling large objects per task
Passing model objects, large datasets, or heavy class instances as arguments causes enormous pickle overhead. Many such objects are not picklable at all and raise

# BAD — SentenceTransformer is not picklable; will raise PicklingError
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")

with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as pool:
    results = list(pool.map(lambda t: model.encode(t), texts))  # crashes

# GOOD — model loaded once per worker via initializer
_model = None

def _init():
    global _model
    _model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")

def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    return _model.encode(text).tolist()

with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4, initializer=_init) as pool:
    results = list(pool.map(embed, texts))  # correct
Mistake 2 — creating a new pool per request
inside a request handler means paying the 100–500 ms spawn cost on every request. Pools must be long-lived application singletons.
Exceptions raised inside a worker are re-raised in the main process when the Future is resolved. Iterating
results without a try/except around each item silently drops failures.
Use an initializer for heavy resources
— load models, compile regexes, and open DB connections in the pool initializer so they are ready before the first task arrives.
— for large iterables, a chunksize of 32–256 batches multiple items per IPC message, reducing round-trip overhead.
— call multiprocessing.set_start_method('spawn') in the main guard if the parent process uses asyncio, CUDA, or OpenMP, to avoid fork-induced corruption.
— for sandbox use cases, call resource.setrlimit() in the initializer to cap CPU time, memory, and file descriptors per worker.
— the futures API integrates cleanly with asyncio, supports exception propagation, and is compatible with the standard context manager protocol.
— register atexit.register(pool.shutdown, wait=True) and a SIGTERM handler to drain in-flight tasks before the process exits.
— a worker killed by the OOM killer silently shrinks the pool. Catch BrokenProcessPool and reinitialise the executor in a retry wrapper.
The theoretical speedup of parallelising a fraction
cores is bounded by Amdahl's law:

If 20% of the pipeline is inherently serial — reading from disk, writing results — the maximum speedup on any number of cores is 5x regardless of worker count. Profile the serial fraction before scaling out workers.
— scale workers up to os.cpu_count(). Hyperthreads help for memory-intensive work but not for pure compute-bound tasks.
— limit workers to floor(available_RAM / model_size). Memory bandwidth is usually the bottleneck before CPU saturation with large models.
— time a no-op worker function. If overhead is more than 10% of task time, increase chunksize or redesign to pass smaller identifiers.
— on NUMA architectures and machines with more than 16 cores, adding workers past the memory bandwidth limit yields diminishing or negative returns.
For workloads that outgrow a single machine,
does not scale horizontally. Distributed task queues (Celery, RQ) or distributed compute frameworks (Ray, Dask) extend the same conceptual model — submit task, receive result — across a cluster.
module is the preferred interface for most use cases. It provides
(results as they finish, unordered). Available without installation.

Ray is an open-source distributed compute framework for ML workloads. It extends the process-pool model to a cluster with minimal code changes. A Ray remote function runs in a separate process or remote node and returns an object reference.

Celery distributes tasks across worker processes (or machines) via a message broker such as Redis or RabbitMQ. It adds durability, scheduling, retries, and rate limiting — making it the standard choice for production agent background jobs that must survive process restarts.
For large numpy arrays, shared memory avoids pickling by mapping the same physical memory into multiple processes. Workers attach by name and read without copying.

from multiprocessing import shared_memory
import numpy as np

# Main process creates the block and populates it
shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(create=True, size=1_000_000 * 4)
arr = np.ndarray((1_000_000,), dtype=np.float32, buffer=shm.buf)
arr[:] = np.random.rand(1_000_000).astype(np.float32)

# Workers receive only the name (a string) — no pickling of array data
def compute_slice(shm_name: str, start: int, end: int) -> float:
    existing = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=shm_name)
    view = np.ndarray((1_000_000,), dtype=np.float32, buffer=existing.buf)
    result = float(view[start:end].mean())
    existing.close()
    return result

# Cleanup
shm.close()
shm.unlink()

Threading does bypass the GIL for blocking C extensions — NumPy BLAS matrix multiplication releases the GIL, so threads can achieve parallel BLAS calls. For pure Python CPU work, threads provide no speedup.

Running untrusted code in a subprocess provides a meaningful isolation boundary but is not a security guarantee on its own. A malicious script can still read files accessible to the worker process, exhaust OS resources, or exploit kernel vulnerabilities. Harden subprocess execution by: running workers as a dedicated low-privilege user, enforcing resource limits with
in the initializer, restricting filesystem access with seccomp or a chroot jail, and enforcing a wall-clock timeout.
do not support cancellation once a task is executing — unlike asyncio Tasks. Use
only for queued tasks that have not yet started. For potentially hanging workers, send SIGTERM to the worker PID or use
— if a worker is killed by the OOM killer, the pool raises BrokenProcessPool on the next submit. Catch it and reinitialise.
— worker exceptions are pickled and re-raised in the main process when Future.result() is called. Always wrap result() in try/except.
— register a signal handler that calls executor.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) to avoid zombie workers during container restarts.
— if the main process holds asyncio resources, CUDA contexts, or SQLAlchemy connection pools at fork time, those resources may be corrupted in the child. Use the spawn start method.

Think of the main process as a kitchen manager overseeing four chefs (workers) in separate, sealed rooms. At the start of the shift, each chef receives a complete pantry setup — the model, the database connection (initializer) — so they are self-sufficient. For each order, the manager writes a recipe card (pickled arguments) and slides it under the door. Each chef cooks independently on their own stove (CPU core) without ever talking to the other chefs. The finished dish slides back under the door (pickled result) and the manager assembles the final output. The lesson: stock each kitchen fully at the start, then send only lightweight instructions with each task.

content: Multiprocessing spawns independent OS processes with their own GIL and heap, enabling true CPU parallelism that threading cannot provide in CPython.

content.text: is the preferred high-level API; pair it with an initializer to load heavy resources once per worker rather than once per task.

content: Pass only small identifiers across the process boundary — not large objects or model weights — to keep IPC overhead negligible relative to compute time.
Integrate with asyncio via loop.run_in_executor() so CPU-bound batch work runs in parallel without blocking the event loop serving concurrent requests.
Use multiprocessing for batch embedding pipelines, heavy document parsing, and isolated code sandboxes; avoid it for per-request work or I/O-bound tasks where asyncio is more efficient.
Set the start method to spawn (not fork) in environments that use asyncio, CUDA, or OpenMP to prevent fork-induced corruption of inherited resources.
Handle BrokenProcessPool, register atexit shutdown hooks, and apply OS resource limits in worker initializers for production reliability and security.
When scale exceeds a single machine, graduate to Ray or Celery — they extend the same task-submission model across a cluster with added durability, scheduling, and auto-restart.
